planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), well-drained to moderately wet soil in part shade to full shade; Florida leucothoe thrives in moist woodland edges, stream banks, and shaded sites where its arching, fountain-like form creates an elegant understory effect.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic to moderately acidic, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; Florida leucothoe thrives in moist, shaded woodland gardens alongside ferns, azaleas, and hostas. Agarista populifolia (Florida leucothoe or tall leucothoe) is a large, arching, semi-evergreen to evergreen native shrub forming an elegant fountain of gracefully nodding branches clothed in lustrous, dark-green, alternate, lance-shaped to ovate leaves; small, white, urn-shaped, sweetly fragrant flowers are produced in pendulous clusters in April–May, covering the branches in a frothy, white-flowered curtain. The overall effect is remarkably elegant for a native shrub, combining refined foliage, fragrant flowers, and an architectural arching form that makes it one of the finest native screening and understory shrubs for the Deep South. Florida leucothoe is found wild in moist woodland ravines and bottomlands from South Carolina to northern Florida and is especially well-adapted to warm, humid, acidic-soil gardens in zones 7–9. Though often confused with the common drooping leucothoe (Leucothoe fontanesiana), Florida leucothoe is substantially larger, more heat-tolerant, and better adapted to zone 8–9 gardens.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained to poorly drained soil in full sun to full shade; shining fetterbush naturally occurs in wet flatwoods, bay swamps, and moist pocosins and is exceptionally tolerant of wet, poorly drained, acidic soils.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to full shade; shining fetterbush performs remarkably well in wet, poorly drained conditions that challenge most ornamental shrubs, making it valuable for rain gardens, pond margins, and wet woodland settings. Lyonia lucida is an evergreen native shrub of the southeastern coastal plain with exceptionally handsome, glossy, dark-green, alternate leaves with a prominent revolute (rolled-under) margin that gives the plant a polished, refined appearance unusual in native shrubs; small, pink to white, urn-shaped flowers are produced in axillary clusters along the previous season's stems in March–April before many other shrubs flower, providing important early-season nectar for native bumblebees and other early-flying pollinators. The glossy foliage (the source of the epithet 'lucida,' meaning shining) is effective year-round and provides excellent winter interest. Shining fetterbush is native to wet flatwoods, bay swamps, and pocosins from Virginia to Florida and Louisiana and is an outstanding native choice for wet, acidic-soil sites in zones 7–10 where drainage is poor.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist to moderately dry, well-drained to somewhat poorly drained soil in full sun to full shade; creeping liriope is far more aggressive and spreading than clumping liriope (Liriope muscari) and is best used where invasive colonization is desired — under trees, on slopes, or in confined beds.

directions: Plant plugs or divisions 12–18 inches apart in spring in virtually any well-drained to moderately moist soil; Liriope spicata spreads vigorously by stolons and quickly forms a solid groundcover mat. Liriope spicata is the strongly spreading (stoloniferous) liriope that forms a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover mat of narrow, grass-like, dark-green foliage; pale-lavender to near-white flower spikes appear in July–August just above or within the foliage and are followed by black berries. Unlike clumping Liriope muscari (the more commonly planted species), L. spicata spreads aggressively by underground stolons and can become weedy in formal settings — it is most appropriate under large trees, on slopes for erosion control, or in large areas where low-maintenance ground coverage is the goal. In deep shade under mature trees where little else grows well, it performs reliably. The cultivar 'Silver Dragon' has white-striped foliage and somewhat less aggressive spread than the green-leaved species. Shear to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges to refresh appearance and remove winter-damaged foliage.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or division clumps in spring in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; pinch stem tips repeatedly through midsummer (until about July 15 in most zones) to encourage bushy growth and maximum flower bud development for fall bloom.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; pinch growing tips every 2–3 weeks from spring through mid-July to encourage branching and maximize fall bloom. Chrysanthemum morifolium is the garden mum — the standard fall-flowering perennial of American horticulture, producing a spectacular flush of daisy-like to button-like to pom-pom flowers in a huge range of colors (white, yellow, bronze, orange, red, pink, purple, lavender) in August–October depending on cultivar. Hardy garden mums are classified by flower form (single, decorative, cushion, pompon, spider, quill, and more) and blooming time (early, mid, late season). In zones 5–7 they are reliably perennial with some protection; in zones 8–9 they may be treated as warm-season annuals that bloom September–November and then decline, as summer heat interferes with vegetative development. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring to maintain vigor. The key cultural requirement is consistent pinching through summer — without it, plants develop long, ungainly stems with few flowers rather than the compact, floriferous mounds depicted in catalogs.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring, summer, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Indian hawthorn is one of the most reliable and widely planted evergreen shrubs in the Gulf South and is extraordinarily tolerant of coastal conditions, salt spray, and poor sandy soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Indian hawthorn tolerates part shade but blooms more heavily and stays more compact with at least 6 hours of direct sun. Rhaphiolepis indica is a rugged, compact, rounded evergreen shrub native to southern China that has become one of the most widely planted landscape shrubs in the Deep South, Gulf Coast, and California for good reason — it combines attractive dark-green, leathery, serrated leaves with clusters of fragrant, 5-petaled, white to deep-pink flowers in spring (and often again in fall), followed by small, blue-black berries that attract birds, all on a compact, tidy plant that requires minimal maintenance. It is outstanding for coastal landscapes — tolerating salt spray, poor sandy soils, and coastal wind exposure that defeats most shrubs. The cultivar 'Eleanor Taber' has deep-pink flowers; 'Ballerina' is compact with rosy-pink flowers; 'Spring Rapture' has rose-red flowers. Indian hawthorn is susceptible to Entomosporium leaf spot (especially in humid climates) — give plants good air circulation and avoid overhead irrigation to minimize this problem. Prune immediately after spring bloom only to shape.
